statement.markdown: The edit distance between two strings is the minimum number of operations (insertions, deletions, and substitutions) required to change one string into the other. Given two strings `word1` and `word2`, return the minimum number of operations needed to convert `word1` to `word2`.

examples.explanation: kitten -> sitting (1) k -> s (2) e -> i (3) n -> g
horse -> ros (1) h ->  (2) o ->  (3) e -> s
intention -> execution (1) i -> e (2) n -> x (3) t -> c (4) e -> u (5) n -> t

hints.text: The problem involves transforming one string into another with the minimum number of operations. Think about how you can break down the problem into smaller subproblems.

Consider using dynamic programming to store and reuse the results of subproblems. The edit distance between two strings can be computed by comparing characters from the end of both strings and working your way backwards.

The core technique here is to use a 2D array to store the edit distances between substrings of `word1` and `word2`. The final solution will involve filling in this array in a bottom-up manner, using the base cases where one of the strings is empty.

editorial.title: Edit Distance: A Dynamic Programming Approach
editorial.intuition: The edit distance problem can be solved by finding the minimum number of operations (insertions, deletions, and substitutions) required to transform one word into another. A brute force approach would involve generating all possible transformations and selecting the one with the minimum cost, but this is highly inefficient.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve generating all possible sequences of operations (insertions, deletions, and substitutions) and selecting the one with the minimum cost. However, this approach is extremely slow, with a time complexity that is exponential in the lengths of the input words.
editorial.betterSolution: The key observation that unlocks the optimal approach is that the edit distance between two words can be computed by considering the edit distances between their prefixes. Specifically, the edit distance between two words is the minimum cost of transforming the last characters of the words, plus the edit distance between the prefixes of the words without the last characters.
editorial.optimalSolution: The dynamic programming approach builds a 2D table where each cell [i][j] represents the edit distance between the first i characters of the first word and the first j characters of the second word. The final edit distance is stored in the bottom-right cell of the table.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the dynamic programming approach can be proved by induction on the lengths of the input words. The base cases are when one or both of the words are empty, in which case the edit distance is simply the length of the non-empty word. For the inductive step, we consider the minimum cost of transforming the last characters of the words, and show that this cost is correctly computed by considering the edit distances between the prefixes of the words.
editorial.dryRun: The dynamic programming approach has been verified to produce the correct results for various test cases, including edge cases such as empty words and words with a single character.
commonMistakes: Not initializing the base cases correctly
Not considering all possible operations (insertions, deletions, and substitutions)
Not using dynamic programming to store and reuse intermediate results
Incorrectly computing the minimum cost of transforming the last characters of the words
Not handling edge cases such as empty words correctly

functionSignature.java: public int editDistance(String word1, String word2);
functionSignature.python: def editDistance(self, word1: str, word2: str) -> int:
